metadata.title: NZ 2026 Policy Tracker
metadata.note: Official-source summaries collected from party websites. Check each source before relying on an entry as a final 2026 manifesto commitment.

policies.title: Economy and cost of living delivery record
policies.summary: National says its current work is focused on rebuilding the economy, easing cost pressures, cutting red tape, supporting trade and investment, and creating conditions for higher incomes.

policies.title: Fiscal plan and tax relief
policies.summary: National's older fiscal plan set out a disciplined-spending, lower-tax, lower-debt approach while maintaining funding for frontline services and infrastructure.

policies.title: Quality, timely healthcare
policies.summary: National says it is delivering more doctors, nurses, medicines, health targets, local urgent care, and easier access to healthcare as part of its current government programme.

policies.title: Teaching the Basics Brilliantly
policies.summary: National says it has mandated daily reading, writing and maths, banned cell-phone use in schools, introduced structured teaching, proposed replacing NCEA, and expanded learning support.

policies.title: Restoring law and order
policies.summary: National says it has reduced violent crime and ram raids, increased police patrols, restricted gang patches, restored Three Strikes, capped sentence discounts, and agreed a methamphetamine harm plan.

policies.title: Blueprint for a Better Environment
policies.summary: National's older environment policy covered freshwater, biodiversity, climate, oceans, waste and planning-law reform, with an emphasis on environmental outcomes alongside growth.

policies.title: Three free doctor's visits with Medicard
policies.summary: Labour says every New Zealander would receive three free doctor's visits each year through a new Medicard, funded by targeted property tax changes.

policies.title: Targeted capital gains tax
policies.summary: Labour proposes a 28% tax on profit made after 1 July 2027 when commercial or residential investment property is sold, excluding the family home, farms, KiwiSaver, shares, businesses, inheritances and personal items.

policies.title: New Zealand Future Fund
policies.summary: Labour says the New Zealand Future Fund would invest in infrastructure and innovative New Zealand businesses to support secure jobs and keep more ideas, wealth and people in New Zealand.

policies.title: Free cervical screening
policies.summary: Labour says cervical screening would be free for people aged 25 to 69, loaded onto the Medicard, and funded from baseline health funding from the 2027/28 financial year.

policies.title: Family Doctor Loan Scheme
policies.summary: Labour proposes up to 50 loans each year to help doctors buy into or establish owner-operated general practices, prioritising areas with no GPs or closed books, with 10-year repayment and interest-free first two years.

policies.title: Flatter tax and less regulation
policies.summary: ACT says it wants careful government spending, lower taxes, less red tape, a two-rate income tax system with a 28% top rate, and fewer barriers to investment, jobs and wage growth.

policies.title: Health accountability and public-private delivery
policies.summary: ACT says health services should be timely and based on individual need, with public-private partnerships, measurable Health NZ targets, transparency, and less bureaucracy.

policies.title: Restoring law and order
policies.summary: ACT says government should protect safety and property, restore consequences for offenders, focus police on frontline crime-fighting, and use rehabilitation incentives for prisoners who gain skills.

policies.title: Education choice and accountability
policies.summary: ACT supports parent choice across public, private and partnership schools, core knowledge in reading, writing, maths, science and history, stronger NCEA accountability, and a Student Education Account system.

policies.title: Infrastructure planning and delivery
policies.summary: ACT says infrastructure should be delivered efficiently with long-term planning, lower red tape, private investment where appropriate, user-pays approaches, property-rights protections, and decentralised decision-making.

policies.title: Policy page and coalition agreement
policies.summary: New Zealand First's policy page currently points readers to its 2023 election policies and to the coalition agreement commitments it says are supported by National and ACT in government.

policies.title: Economic coalition achievements
policies.summary: New Zealand First lists achieved coalition commitments including a banking competition inquiry, assessing inflation's effect on average tax rates by or before 2026, and increased funding for IRD tax audits.

policies.title: Law and order 2023 policy
policies.summary: NZ First's 2023 policy proposed at least 500 new frontline police in the first 18 months, more Youth Aid officers, mandatory sentences for assaults on first responders, and stronger sentencing policies.

policies.title: Health and PHARMAC 2023 policy
policies.summary: NZ First's 2023 health policy proposed revisiting GP funding, increasing primary-care funding, mobile health buses, more diagnostic access, and a larger PHARMAC budget with rapid access funding for innovative medicines.

policies.title: Education and skills 2023 policy
policies.summary: NZ First's 2023 policy emphasised core skills, learning support, links between education and employment, STEM incentives, third-year fees-free tertiary study, and free apprenticeships for in-demand vocations.

policies.title: Agriculture and emissions 2023 policy
policies.summary: NZ First's 2023 agriculture policy opposed agriculture emissions pricing unless adopted by trading partners, supported farm-level reporting, and proposed incentives for emissions-reduction technologies.

policies.title: Green Budget 2025
policies.summary: The Green Budget 2025 sets out an alternative budget with free GPs and dental, free early childhood education, nature restoration, clean energy, whenua return, and a fairer tax system to fund it.

policies.title: Fiscal Strategy
policies.summary: The Green fiscal strategy argues for fiscal management focused on wellbeing, climate resilience, public investment, economic sovereignty, and recognising the costs of underinvestment.

policies.title: Health Policy
policies.summary: The Green health policy calls for preventive care, reducing disparities, fully funded public provision of dental care, GP clinics, ambulance, emergency, aged, palliative and mental-health services, plus fair conditions for health workers.

policies.title: Education Policy
policies.summary: The Green education policy supports inclusive, accessible and democratic education, free quality education, Te Tiriti-centred learning, fully funded free ECE, fees-free tertiary education, and writing off student loan debt.

policies.title: Climate Change Policy
policies.summary: The Green climate policy calls for Te Tiriti-based climate solutions, energy sufficiency and democratisation, sustainable transport, phasing out fossil fuels, agricultural diversification, adaptation planning, and just transitions.

policies.title: Livelihoods Policy
policies.summary: The Green livelihoods policy supports liveable income support, accessible welfare services, a legal right to a minimum income, protections against exploitative lending, and basic services that support wellbeing.

policies.title: 2026 policy platform status
policies.summary: Te Pāti Māori's main policy page currently says the party is working on its policies and will be back soon, so current 2026 policy entries should be reviewed when the platform is published.

policies.title: Mana Motuhake
policies.summary: The Mana Motuhake policy calls for constitutional transformation, a Māori Parliament, implementing Matike Mai recommendations, recognising Aotearoa as the official name, land return measures, binding Waitangi Tribunal recommendations, and stronger Te Tiriti oversight.

policies.title: Education and training
policies.summary: Te Pāti Māori's education policy centres on resourcing kaupapa Māori education, overhauling the mainstream system, directing more budget to Māori models of delivery, free digital access, and pathways through STEM, trades and apprenticeships.

policies.title: Prisons position statement
policies.summary: A January 2026 Te Pāti Māori statement says the party stands behind its policy of creating an Aotearoa where prisons are not needed, arguing prisons do not address root causes such as poverty, inequality, unemployment, homelessness, racism, poor mental health and trauma.

policies.title: Call to publish gender-affirming care guidelines
policies.summary: Te Pāti Māori has called for Te Whatu Ora to publish updated gender-affirming healthcare guidelines, saying clinical guidance should be led by experts and that withholding the guidelines causes harm to trans and non-binary people.

officialSource.label: Te Pāti Māori gender-affirming care statement

policies.title: 2026 State of the Nation response
policies.summary: Te Pāti Māori's January 2026 response to the Prime Minister's State of the Nation speech argues the Government is building a post-Treaty state and criticises the absence of Te Tiriti, tangata whenua, rangatiratanga, iwi, hapū, whānau, kaitiakitanga and mana motuhake from the speech.

officialSource.label: Te Pāti Māori State of the Nation response
